subject: [PATCH v3 0/3] arm64: cross-CPU NMI via SDEI

reviews.author: Puranjay Mohan
reviews.summary: The reviewer noted that the initcall for ARM_SDEI_NMI runs unconditionally on arm64 systems without SDEI support, causing two warning messages to be printed during boot. They suggested either reducing the severity of the error message or skipping the probe when SDEI is not present.
reviews.raw_body:
This initcall runs unconditionally whenever ARM_SDEI_NMI is built in,
which includes the many arm64 systems that have no SDEI at all. On
those, sdei_event_register() -> sdei_event_create() ->
invoke_sdei_fn() returns -EIO, and the core already complains:
    pr_warn("Failed to create event %u: %d\n", event_num, err);

(that one isn't gated on err != -EIO, unlike sdei_mask_local_cpu() & friends).
We then add a second pr_err() on top, so every boot on a non-SDEI box
with this config gets two alarming lines for what is just "no firmware
support". At minimum, don't shout for -EIO/-EOPNOTSUPP here. Better,
skip the probe when SDEI isn't present  there's no exported predicate
today, but -EIO is the de-facto one.

reviews.author: Puranjay Mohan
reviews.summary: Reviewer noted that panic_smp_self_stop() passes a NULL pointer to crash_save_cpu(), leading to a synchronous abort on arm64 due to dereferencing of regs, and suggested adding a check for NULL regs and either calling crash_setup_regs() or skipping the save if self-stop registers aren't worth having.

reviews.author: Kiryl Shutsemau (author)
reviews.summary:
The author acknowledged a NULL dereference bug and promised to fix it in version 4 of the patch.

The author addressed a concern about the crash_save_cpu() function being called when the interrupted context is NULL, agreeing to drop the 'NULL means capture current' wording and instead only call it if regs is not NULL.
reviews.raw_body:
Good catch, you're right \u2014 that's a real NULL deref, and a regression
from folding ipi_cpu_crash_stop() in (the old code only ran on the IPI
path, which always has regs).

Will be fixed in v4.

---

I went with skipping it. No architecture saves registers for the
self-stopping CPU -- the generic, arm and powerpc panic_smp_self_stop()
all just mark offline and spin, and arm64 did the same via
local_cpu_stop() before this series. crash_save_cpu() is only ever fed
the interrupted context, from the crash shootdown or the crashing CPU
itself. So:

	if (crash && regs)
		crash_save_cpu(regs, cpu);

and I dropped the "NULL means capture current" wording from the comment
rather than make it true.

While fixing this I noticed the same block broke the CONFIG_KEXEC_CORE=n
build: the unification replaced the original ipi_cpu_crash_stop()'s
#ifdef CONFIG_KEXEC_CORE with an IS_ENABLED() check, but <linux/kexec.h>
only declares crash_save_cpu() under CONFIG_KEXEC_CORE (and pulling in
<linux/crash_core.h> directly collides with kexec.h's own stubs), so the
dead-but-compiled call was an implicit-declaration error. Restored the
#ifdef around the crash_save_cpu() call.

Thanks for the review!

-- 
  Kiryl Shutsemau / Kirill A. Shutemov

reviews.author: Kiryl Shutsemau (author)
reviews.summary: Author acknowledged a concern about SDEI support and agreed to add a check for its presence before initializing the NMI mechanism, planning to gate sdei_nmi_init() on it.
reviews.raw_body:
Fair enough. I will add sdei_is_present() and gate sdei_nmi_init() on
it.

-- 
  Kiryl Shutsemau / Kirill A. Shutemov

2026-06-15.patch_summary: This patch series adds a new mechanism to deliver NMI (Non-Maskable Interrupt) across CPUs on arm64 architecture, using the SDEI (System Description Event Infrastructure) protocol. The goal is to enable interrupting a CPU that has its interrupts locally masked, which is necessary for certain debug and observability features such as system backtraces and crash dumps. The existing pseudo-NMI mechanism is too expensive in terms of performance overhead, so this new approach uses SDEI events delivered by firmware to reach the target CPU without modifying the interrupt mask/unmask hot path. This patch series does not add a hard-lockup detector but provides a way for the backtrace and crash-stop paths to actually reach a masked CPU.

reviews.author: Doug Anderson
reviews.summary:
Reviewer Doug Anderson noted that the SDEI event signal call sdei_event_signal() is not properly synchronized, which could lead to a race condition when delivering an NMI to a CPU.

Reviewer Doug Anderson suggested using kernel-doc format for function comments in this file, noting that all other functions follow this convention and it wouldn't introduce any issues.

Reviewer Doug Anderson noted that the patch lacks a necessary memory barrier (smp_rmb()) to match the existing smp_wmb(), which is required for correct ordering of operations.
reviews.raw_body:
Hi,

On Sun, Jun 14, 2026 at 7:36 PM Kiryl Shutsemau <kirill@shutemov.name> wrote:

---

Nice to have all the details in the function comment. Any reason why
you didn't use kernel-doc format? Nothing else in this file does, I
guess, but it doesn't seem like it would be a problem to start the
trend... ;-)

---

Don't you need a smp_rmb() before that, to match with the smp_wmb()?

-Doug

2026-06-16.patch_summary: This patch series adds a new mechanism to deliver NMI (Non-Maskable Interrupt) across CPUs on arm64 architecture, using the SDEI (System Description Event Infrastructure) protocol. The goal is to enable interrupting a CPU that has its interrupts locally masked, which is necessary for certain debug and observability features such as system backtraces and crash dumps. The existing pseudo-NMI mechanism is too expensive in terms of performance overhead, so this new approach uses SDEI events delivered by firmware to reach the target CPU without modifying the interrupt mask/unmask hot path. This patch series does not add a hard-lockup detector but provides a way for the backtrace and crash-stop paths to actually reach a masked CPU.
